data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Older adult female
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/24 (pinhole 6/12); OS 6/6
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 15 mmHg; OS 14 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the clinical presentation and slit-lamp findings. Identify the complications, discuss your differential diagnosis, and outline your immediate surgical and medical management plan.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st1_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st1_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Explain the optical problem and distinguish IOL rotation from incorrect power selection or corneal causes.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: clinically significant toric intraocular lens malrotation with residual refractive 
astigmatism.Approximately one-third of toric cylinder effect is lost for each 10 degrees of misalignment; near 30 
degrees the intended cylindrical correction is effectively lost and larger errors may induce cylinder on 
a new axis.Causes include large capsular bag, high axial length, retained OVD, incomplete overlap of the optic, 
early postoperative rotation and later capsular contraction. Confirm regular corneal astigmatism and 
exclude dry eye, keratoconus, posterior corneal power error, macular disease, IOL tilt/decentration and 
incorrect biometry.

subQuestions.text: What clinic-based measurements are required before returning to theatre?
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Repeat manifest refraction after ocular-surface optimisation; obtain keratometry/topography or 
tomography, optical biometry and macular OCT.Dilate and document the current IOL axis at the slit lamp or with photography. Compare implanted 
model/power/axis with the operative record and recalculate using contemporary toric planning that 
includes posterior corneal astigmatism and surgically induced astigmatism.Use vector analysis or a toric back-calculator to decide whether rotation alone, IOL exchange or 
corneal laser enhancement best minimises residual cylinder.

subQuestions.text: Give an escalating plan, operative technique and timing considerations.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Optimise the surface and offer spectacles/contact lens if acceptable. Small stable errors can be treated 
with corneal laser only after refraction stabilises and retinal/corneal suitability is confirmed.For a correctly powered lens with substantial axis error, reopen the incisions, fill the chamber and bag 
with OVD, carefully free capsular adhesions, rotate to the calculated axis, remove OVD completely 
including behind the optic, and confirm alignment at physiologic pressure.Exchange the IOL if cylinder power is inappropriate or rotation cannot achieve the target. Early 
intervention is easier; late fibrotic fixation increases zonular/capsular injury and may require optic 
capture, secondary fixation or accepting an alternative correction. Discuss rotation recurrence, PCR, 
CME, infection and retinal detachment.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Adult male
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/12; OS counting fingers at 1 m
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 13 mmHg; OS digital estimate only
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the slit-lamp findings and any corneal imaging provided. Describe the abnormalities, state the most likely diagnosis, and discuss your immediate treatment strategy.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st2_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st2_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Describe the characteristic morphology and distinguish the closest causes of peripheral thinning.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: Terrien marginal degeneration complicated by corneal perforation.Classically painless, non-inflammatory superior peripheral stromal thinning spreads circumferentially 
with intact epithelium, superficial radial vessels and a sharp lipid line at the central edge; progressive 
flattening induces against-the-rule or oblique astigmatism.Mooren ulcer and systemic PUK are painful and inflamed with epithelial loss/overhanging edge; 
pellucid degeneration is inferior and avascular without lipid; dellen, marginal keratitis, sclerokeratitis 
and infectious melt must also be excluded.

subQuestions.text: Which tests quantify severity and when is systemic work-up appropriate?
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Seidel testing without pressure on the globe, slit-lamp photography, pachymetric mapping, anterior-
segment OCT and corneal tomography/topography; avoid tonometry and gonioscopy in an open globe.Assess both eyes and document epithelial integrity, vascularisation, lipid, extent and minimum 
residual thickness. Culture any epithelial defect or infiltrate.Typical quiet Terrien degeneration needs no broad laboratory screen; inflammation, ulceration or 
scleritis warrants targeted autoimmune and infectious investigation including rheumatoid vasculitis, 
ANCA-associated disease, syphilis and TB according to context.

subQuestions.text: Manage the perforation and subsequent optical rehabilitation.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Protect with rigid shield, nil by mouth, antiemetic/analgesia and systemic/topical antimicrobial cover; 
avoid pressure, patching and topical steroid until infection is excluded. Small focal leaks may be 
temporised with cyanoacrylate and bandage lens.Definitive repair depends on size and geometry: multilayer amniotic membrane for selected defects, 
tectonic lamellar patch or crescentic lamellar keratoplasty, or penetrating/mini-penetrating graft when 
full-thickness central extension or iris/lens involvement prevents lamellar repair.Later use spectacles, contact lens or carefully selected keratoplasty for irregular astigmatism. Avoid 
corneal cross-linking in extremely thin tissue and provide lifelong protective eyewear and monitoring 
of the fellow eye.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Adult male
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/6; OS 6/18
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 15 mmHg; OS 54 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the clinical presentation and imaging findings. Identify the pathology, discuss the mechanism, and outline your immediate and long-term management plan.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st3_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st3_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Explain the disproportionate pressure rise and differentiate its important mimics.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: Posner-Schlossman syndrome (glaucomatocyclitic crisis), with CMV anterior 
uveitis requiring consideration.Recurrent trabeculitis markedly reduces outflow despite mild cyclitis. CMV is an important association, 
particularly with recurrent high IOP, coin-shaped KPs, endothelial loss or iris atrophy.Differentiate HSV/VZV anterior uveitis, Fuchs uveitis, steroid response, pigment dispersion, 
pseudoexfoliation and acute angle closure. Gonioscopy must confirm an open angle and exclude 
PAS/neovascularisation.

subQuestions.text: When and how will you investigate?
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
During an active untreated episode obtain a small aqueous sample for CMV, HSV and VZV PCR; 
Goldmann-Witmer coefficient may help when PCR is negative but suspicion persists. Do not delay 
urgent pressure control.Document KPs, iris transillumination/atrophy, endothelial count and pachymetry. Perform disc 
photography, OCT RNFL/GCC and fields after IOP is safer, recognising that repeated attacks can cause 
permanent glaucoma.Review steroid exposure, immune status and recurrence pattern; routine broad systemic inflammatory 
testing is usually low yield unless phenotype is atypical.

subQuestions.text: Give acute treatment, virus-directed therapy and surgical escalation.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Use topical aqueous suppressants promptly; add oral acetazolamide if safe and hyperosmotic therapy 
for an immediately threatened nerve. Treat inflammation with topical corticosteroid and taper 
according to cells, avoiding miotics.For proven/suspected CMV, involve a uveitis specialist and use topical or systemic 
valganciclovir/ganciclovir according to severity, recurrence and local availability; monitor FBC and 
renal function for systemic therapy and counsel about relapse after cessation.If cumulative glaucomatous damage or uncontrolled IOP persists, select surgery according to angle and 
inflammation. Trabeculectomy with antimetabolite or a drainage implant may be required; angle 
surgery is an option in selected open-angle eyes. Control viral/inflammatory activity perioperatively.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Adult male
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/6; OS 6/60
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 14 mmHg; OS 13 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the clinical presentation and fundus findings. Describe the inflammatory phenotype, state the diagnosis, and outline your investigation and treatment plan.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st4_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st4_img2.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 3](/images/volume6/vol6_st4_img3.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Integrate the colour, autofluorescence and OCT findings and distinguish key mimics.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: acute syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis (ocular syphilis).A large placoid yellow-grey lesion at the posterior pole may show stippled hyperautofluorescence. OCT 
demonstrates nodular/thickened RPE, disruption of ellipsoid/external limiting membrane, subretinal 
fluid and hyperreflective choroidal foci; changes can reverse with treatment.Differentiate APMPPE/ampiginous disease, serpiginous choroiditis, VKH, central serous 
chorioretinopathy, lymphoma and AZOOR. Syphilis can mimic any uveitic phenotype, and steroid 
monotherapy may worsen or obscure it.

subQuestions.text: Specify serology, infectious assessment and when lumbar puncture is needed.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Obtain both a treponemal test and quantitative non-treponemal titre (RPR/VDRL); a single test is 
insufficient. Test for HIV and other sexually transmitted infections and involve infectious 
diseases/sexual health for partner notification.Ocular syphilis is treated as neurosyphilis irrespective of CSF findings. Lumbar puncture is indicated 
when neurologic signs are present and may establish baseline CSF abnormalities, but must not delay 
therapy.Document OCT, FAF and photography; FA/ICGA can support activity. Exclude TB and other infection 
before immunosuppression if the presentation is atypical.

subQuestions.text: State the definitive regimen and follow-up principles.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Give aqueous crystalline penicillin G intravenously at neurosyphilis dosing for 10–14 days; an accepted 
alternative is procaine penicillin plus probenecid when appropriate. Coordinate allergy 
evaluation/desensitisation when necessary.Warn about Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction. Use topical steroid/cycloplegia for anterior inflammation and 
reserve systemic corticosteroid for selected severe inflammation only after effective antibiotic therapy 
has begun.Follow vision, inflammation and OCT/FAF response plus quantitative RPR/VDRL decline at 
recommended intervals. Assess reinfection or treatment failure if titres fail to fall; counsel regarding 
transmission, partner treatment and HIV prevention.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Older adult female
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/12; OS 6/24
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 14 mmHg; OS 15 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the fundus photographs and optical coherence tomography. Describe the clinical findings, discuss the mechanism of the pathology, and outline your management options.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st5_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st5_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Explain the lesion and separate it from choroidal melanoma and other pseudomelanomas.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: peripheral exudative haemorrhagic chorioretinopathy (PEHCR).Typically elderly and temporal, with peripheral subretinal/sub-RPE haemorrhage, haemorrhagic or 
serous PED, lipid, fibrosis and RPE hyperplasia/atrophy. It may be bilateral and associated with 
AMD/PCV-like choroidal vasculopathy.Melanoma is a solid choroidal mass with low-to-medium internal reflectivity, acoustic hollowness, 
intrinsic vascularity, orange pigment or subretinal fluid; also consider retinal macroaneurysm, 
choroidal haemangioma/metastasis, detachment and peripheral CNV.

subQuestions.text: What multimodal imaging establishes the diagnosis and what systemic factors matter?
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Use widefield colour/red-free photography, peripheral OCT if technically possible, B-scan and A-scan. 
FA can show blockage/window defects; ICGA may reveal polypoidal lesions and helps distinguish 
tumour vascularity.Document dimensions and serial change. Refer to ocular oncology when features are not classic, 
growth occurs or a solid mass cannot be excluded; biopsy is rarely first-line.Review hypertension, anticoagulants/antiplatelets and bleeding risk with the prescribing clinician—do 
not stop life-saving therapy unilaterally. Examine the fellow eye and macula for AMD/PCV.

subQuestions.text: Give management according to symptoms and complications.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Observe most asymptomatic, stable peripheral lesions with serial imaging because spontaneous 
involution is common. Optimise systemic vascular risk.Use intravitreal anti-VEGF for macula-threatening exudation/haemorrhage or active peripheral 
neovascular leakage; laser or cryotherapy is selective when a discrete leaking peripheral focus is 
visible and tumour has been excluded.For non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage, traction/rhegmatogenous detachment or massive 
breakthrough haemorrhage, perform PPV with treatment of causative lesions and tamponade as 
needed. Consider pneumatic displacement/tPA strategies only for clinically significant recent 
submacular blood after individual risk assessment.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Adult female
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/6; OS 6/36
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 14 mmHg; OS 13 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the clinical presentation and neuroimaging. Describe the abnormalities, localise the lesion, and outline your immediate investigation and management priorities.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st6_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st6_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Localise the process and distinguish inflammatory granuloma from tumour and infection.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: optic nerve head/peripapillary granuloma due to ocular sarcoidosis causing optic 
neuropathy.Disc swelling plus an elevated peripapillary choroidal/retinal lesion, vitreous cells and subretinal fluid 
localise an infiltrative granulomatous process at the nerve head/adjacent choroid.Differential includes tuberculoma, syphilis, toxocariasis, fungal infection, choroidal 
melanoma/metastasis, lymphoma, optic nerve sheath meningioma and idiopathic inflammatory 
pseudotumour. Immunosuppression before excluding infection can be catastrophic.

subQuestions.text: How will you establish the cause while protecting vision?
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
MRI brain/orbits with contrast and fat suppression assesses nerve, sheath, apex and CNS disease. OCT 
RNFL/GCC and macula, fields, colour vision, photography and B-scan establish baseline anatomy and 
function.Chest CT, ACE/lysozyme only as supportive tests, calcium, FBC/U&E/LFT and ECG; screen for TB and 
syphilis. Seek the safest tissue diagnosis—EBUS lymph-node, skin or conjunctival lesion—rather than 
biopsy of the optic nerve.Apply contemporary ocular sarcoidosis criteria and coordinate respiratory/rheumatology care. Urgent 
biopsy is required if malignancy remains plausible or the response is atypical.

subQuestions.text: Give acute treatment and steroid-sparing escalation.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
For sight-threatening optic nerve involvement, give high-dose systemic corticosteroid—often IV 
methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisone—after appropriate infectious exclusion, with 
gastric/bone and metabolic prophylaxis.Introduce early steroid-sparing therapy for relapse, bilateral disease or steroid toxicity: methotrexate, 
mycophenolate, azathioprine or biologic therapy such as adalimumab/infliximab in refractory disease, 
with screening and laboratory monitoring.Treat associated anterior/intermediate uveitis locally as appropriate and monitor acuity, colour, fields, 
OCT, lesion size and systemic organ involvement. Failure to improve requires reconsideration of 
infection, lymphoma or another mass.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Adult male
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/12; OS 6/6
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 22 mmHg; OS 20 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the motility photographs and any neuroimaging provided. Describe the abnormalities, identify the clinical syndrome, and outline your immediate and long-term management.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st7_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st7_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Explain the motility pattern, disease activity and the mechanism of visual loss.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: thyroid eye disease with restrictive strabismus and dysthyroid optic neuropathy.Inferior rectus restriction causes hypotropia and limited elevation; medial rectus restriction causes 
esotropia/limited abduction. Tendon sparing and muscle-belly enlargement are typical.Optic neuropathy results from apical muscle crowding and/or stretch, and can occur without marked 
proptosis. Assess clinical activity separately from severity; reduced acuity/colour, RAPD, field loss, disc 
change and apical crowding are red flags.

subQuestions.text: What measurements and investigations determine emergency treatment?
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Record acuity, colour plates/red saturation, pupils, fields, optic discs/OCT, IOP in primary and upgaze, 
corneal exposure, Hertel measurements, lid position, ductions and prism deviation.Orbital CT or MRI demonstrates muscle enlargement, apical crowding and optic nerve stretch and 
excludes mass/inflammation. Check thyroid function and receptor antibodies, while recognising 
euthyroid disease.Assess smoking, diabetes and suitability for high-dose steroids; coordinate endocrinology. Corneal 
breakdown or definite optic neuropathy is sight-threatening disease requiring same-day treatment.

subQuestions.text: Give the treatment sequence from optic nerve rescue to strabismus surgery.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Give IV methylprednisolone promptly for dysthyroid optic neuropathy unless contraindicated, with 
monitoring of liver, cardiovascular and glucose risk. If response is inadequate or relapse occurs, 
perform urgent orbital decompression; orbital radiotherapy or biologic therapy is selected in specialist 
multidisciplinary care.Protect exposure with lubrication, moisture chamber, lid closure procedures as required; stop smoking 
and achieve euthyroidism. Active moderate-severe disease may require immunomodulation according 
to current protocols.Rehabilitative surgery waits for inactivity and stable measurements: decompression first, then 
restrictive strabismus surgery using large adjustable recessions guided by forced ductions, then eyelid 
surgery. Counsel that decompression can alter diplopia.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Young child female
**Visual Acuity:** OD fixes and follows; OS poor fixation
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 18 mmHg; OS 32 mmHg under anaesthesia
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the clinical presentation and imaging findings. State the diagnosis, outline the key risks, and discuss your investigation and treatment plan.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st8_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st8_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Explain the ocular enlargement and distinguish primary from secondary childhood glaucoma.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: primary congenital glaucoma with buphthalmos and Haab striae, worse in the left 
eye.Developmental trabecular outflow abnormality raises IOP while cornea and sclera remain elastic, 
producing enlarged corneal diameter, axial elongation, deep chamber, Descemet tears, oedema and 
potentially reversible early cupping.Exclude anterior-segment dysgenesis, aniridia, Sturge-Weber, uveitis, trauma/non-accidental injury, 
retinopathy of prematurity treatment, aphakia and steroid exposure. Megalocornea has clear 
symmetric large corneas, normal IOP/axial length and no glaucomatous cupping.

subQuestions.text: Specify the EUA dataset and genetic/systemic assessment.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Measure IOP early under a standardised anaesthetic, horizontal corneal diameter, pachymetry, axial 
length, refraction, gonioscopy, optic disc photography and corneal clarity/Haab striae in both eyes.Assess amblyopia, nystagmus and fixation. Repeat axial length, refraction and disc documentation to 
judge control because office IOP alone is unreliable.Take a three-generation pedigree and offer genetic counselling/testing, particularly CYP1B1 and other 
childhood glaucoma genes in bilateral, familial or consanguineous cases; examine siblings and assess 
systemic features when secondary syndromes are possible.

subQuestions.text: Give the operative hierarchy and visual rehabilitation plan.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Medical aqueous suppression is a bridge, not definitive primary therapy. Perform goniotomy when the 
cornea is clear and angle visible, or trabeculotomy ab externo/360-degree catheter trabeculotomy 
when the cornea is cloudy or disease extensive.Repeat/combined trabeculotomy-trabeculectomy may be required; drainage implant or 
cyclodestruction is reserved for refractory disease with careful lifetime complication planning. Treat 
corneal oedema by controlling IOP; keratoplasty is rarely the first step.Correct myopia/astigmatism promptly, institute amblyopia therapy and provide lifelong surveillance 
for pressure, axial growth, cupping, cataract and surgical complications.

data.scenario:
**Demographics:** Adult female
**Visual Acuity:** OD 6/6; OS 6/18
**Intraocular Pressure:** OD 14 mmHg; OS 15 mmHg
**Primary Instruction:** Examine the clinical presentation and orbital imaging. Describe the abnormalities, state the diagnosis, and discuss your immediate and long-term management plan.

**Clinical Images:**
![Clinical Image 1](/images/volume6/vol6_st9_img1.jpeg)
![Clinical Image 2](/images/volume6/vol6_st9_img2.jpeg)

subQuestions.text: Explain why the lesion is malignant and distinguish other lacrimal fossa masses.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Definitive diagnosis: adenoid cystic carcinoma of the lacrimal gland.Pain from early perineural invasion, relatively rapid progression, irregular/infiltrative margins, bone 
destruction and sensory change favour malignancy. Inferonasal globe displacement localises a 
superotemporal extraconal mass.Pleomorphic adenoma is usually painless, well circumscribed and causes smooth remodelling; 
lymphoma moulds around structures; dacryoadenitis is inflammatory and often bilateral; consider 
carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma, metastasis and epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma.

subQuestions.text: Plan tissue diagnosis without compromising definitive surgery.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Obtain thin-slice contrast MRI of orbit/skull base to map perineural spread and intracranial extension 
plus CT for bone. Stage neck/nodes and distant disease—especially lungs—using appropriate cross-
sectional or PET imaging.In a lesion suspicious for adenoid cystic carcinoma, obtain a carefully planned incisional/core biopsy 
along a tract that can be excised; avoid violating multiple tissue planes. By contrast, do not biopsy a 
classic encapsulated pleomorphic adenoma before intact excision.Pathology should report cribriform/solid pattern, margins and perineural invasion; multidisciplinary 
orbital oncology, head-and-neck, radiation oncology and pathology review is essential.

subQuestions.text: Give definitive local treatment and address recurrence risk.
subQuestions.modelAnswer:
Treatment is en bloc surgical resection with negative margins where feasible, ranging from globe-
sparing lacrimal/orbital resection to exenteration and cranio-orbital resection according to extent; 
sacrifice involved nerve/bone when required for oncologic clearance.Adjuvant radiotherapy—often proton or other highly conformal treatment—is commonly used for 
perineural invasion, close margins or advanced disease. Systemic therapy has limited established 
curative benefit but may be used for unresectable/metastatic disease or trials.Counsel regarding dry eye, diplopia, sensory loss, disfigurement and radiation toxicity. Surveillance 
must be prolonged with orbital/skull-base imaging and chest assessment because late local recurrence 
and pulmonary metastasis are characteristic.
